item.title: GOLF CHARITY EVENT | Vision beyond blindness
item.description: From the neonatal ward of Lautoka Hospital to the lecture halls of the University of the South Pacific, and from the quiet village of Somosomo to international training in Japan, the journeys of Sisi Coalala and Veronica Angeline Narayan shine as powerful testaments to resilience and hope. Their stories highlight the transformative work of the Fiji Society for the Blind (FSB). Born prematurely and
item.content:encoded:
From the neonatal ward of Lautoka Hospital to the lecture halls of the University of the South Pacific, and from the quiet village of Somosomo to international training in Japan, the journeys of Sisi Coalala and Veronica Angeline Narayan shine as powerful testaments to resilience and hope. Their stories highlight the transformative work of the Fiji Society for the Blind (FSB).

Born prematurely and diagnosed with progressive vision loss as a toddler, Sisi’s parents faced daunting uncertainty. At just three years old, she began receiving visits from FSB’s Community-Based Rehabilitation Unit. Field workers guided her parents and introduced activities to aid her development. By age five, she was enrolled at the School for the Blind, mastering Braille literacy, independent living skills, and orientation with a white cane.

After five years of foundational training, Sisi was integrated into mainstream education, completing her studies with brailled textbooks provided by FSB. She later attended Saint Joseph’s Secondary School before becoming the first visually impaired student from FSB to be offered a place at the University of the South Pacific. Her academic journey soared further with a Master of Commerce and a Master of HR Management from the University of Wollongong. Today, she serves as Disability Coordinator at Fiji National University and proudly sits as a Board Member of FSB.

Reflecting on her journey, Sisi says: “Without the invaluable support and tireless guidance of the Society’s staff and teachers, I would not be where I am today. Kudos to one and all – you have made a difference not only in my life but in the lives of many of my blind and visually impaired colleagues.”

Born totally blind in Somosomo, Taveuni, Veronica’s life changed when she was introduced to FSB in 1985. With their support, she began her education at Saint Marcellin Primary School, where she learned Braille using a Perkins Braille Machine. “Braille is my pen and paper for life,” she recalls, acknowledging FSB’s role in opening doors to education.

Her journey was not without challenges. At Cathedral Secondary School, Veronica often relied on teachers and classmates dictating notes while she brailled alongside them. Despite setbacks in examinations, FSB never let her falter. She later joined the School for the Blind’s Pre-Vocational program, where she learned computer skills, cooking, sewing, and even attended the Duskin Leadership Course in Japan, mastering Japanese Braille and traditional massage.

In 2008, Veronica began working as a braillist in FSB’s Braille Production Unit, a role she continues today with pride. More recently, she enrolled at Fiji National University to pursue a Diploma in Special and Inclusive Education. Her words capture her gratitude: “Without the Society for the Blind, I will really end up nowhere! FSB is my one and only institution.”

FSB continues to provide critical services – education, rehabilitation, integration, and advocacy. With an annual operating cost of approximately $350,000, fundraising remains vital to sustain these programs.

Barbara Farouk, who coordinates FSB’s fundraising and outreach efforts, speaks to the urgency of continued support: “Every dollar raised goes directly toward giving a child the chance to read, to learn, and to dream. Sisi and Veronica are not exceptions – they are proof of what becomes possible when we refuse to leave anyone behind. We need the community to stand with us so we can keep opening those doors.”

This year, FSB is hosting its Annual Charity Golf Tournament in partnership with Pirates Golf Club on 15 August 2026, aiming to raise $40,000 to support the School for the Blind, hostel facilities, and community outreach programs.

The event has already garnered incredible backing from the local business community, with generous sponsorships secured from leading organisations including the Fiji Times, Vaerua Fishing, Marsh, S Nagindas, Standss, R C Manubhai, Tanoa Hotels, Munro Leys, Eyesite Optometrist and Shipping Services.

Your generosity ensures that children like Sisi and Veronica – and countless others – can access the tools and training they need to live independent, dignified lives.

For sponsorships or donations, contact Barbara Farouk at fjsb@connect.com.fj or call 922 1263 / 338 2966.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Fiji Golf Classic set for Denarau
item.description: THE stage is set for the 2026 Fiji Golf Classic, which will be held from July 30 to August 1 at the picturesque Denarau Golf and Racquet Club. Organised by the Fiji Professional Golfers Association (PGA), the tournament is expected to attract some of the country’s top golfers and overseas participants, promising three days of exciting competition on one of Fiji’s premier golf courses.
item.content:encoded:
THE stage is set for the 2026 Fiji Golf Classic, which will be held from July 30 to August 1 at the picturesque Denarau Golf and Racquet Club.

Organised by the Fiji Professional Golfers Association (PGA), the tournament is expected to attract some of the country’s top golfers and overseas participants, promising three days of exciting competition on one of Fiji’s premier golf courses.

Tournament organisers have also released the rules and conditions for the event, confirming that in the event of a tie after three rounds, the champion will be decided through a three-hole playoff on the 18th, 10th and 18th holes. If players remain tied after the playoff, sudden-death play will continue on the 18th hole until a winner is determined.

Players have also been reminded of local rules regarding out-of-bounds areas, penalty areas and relief for abnormal course conditions, including artificially surfaced roads, irrigation works and construction areas.

The Fiji Golf Classic continues to grow in stature and is expected to provide a major boost for golf in the country, while also promoting sports tourism with Denarau once again serving as a world-class host venue.

Golf enthusiasts and supporters are expected to turn out in numbers to witness some of Fiji’s finest golfers battle for top honours in what promises to be a thrilling tournament.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: RKS ready for all-grades Eastern Zone finals
item.description: THE under-18 final between Ratu Kadavulevu School (RKS) and Lelean Memorial School will headline Saturday’s Eastern Zone Secondary Schools Rugby finals, with the prestigious Lutubula Trophy adding further significance to the highly anticipated clash. RKS, the defending Vodafone Deans champions, will be aiming to continue its strong tradition in secondary schools rugby when it faces an unbeaten Lel
item.content:encoded:
THE under-18 final between Ratu Kadavulevu School (RKS) and Lelean Memorial School will headline Saturday’s Eastern Zone Secondary Schools Rugby finals, with the prestigious Lutubula Trophy adding further significance to the highly anticipated clash.

RKS, the defending Vodafone Deans champions, will be aiming to continue its strong tradition in secondary schools rugby when it faces an unbeaten Lelean side that has impressed throughout the season.

The clash is expected to be a major test for both teams, with RKS looking to defend it dominance while Lelean aims to cap off its unbeaten run with the Eastern Zone title.

Beyond the Under-18 division, RKS has enjoyed a remarkable campaign, with all its grades securing places in Saturday’s finals, highlighting the depth and strength of the school’s rugby program.

The U14 side will face Queen Victoria School (QVS), while the U15 team will battle Lelean Memorial School.

RKS will again meet QVS in the U16 and U17 finals, setting up two exciting encounters between two of Eastern Zone’s traditional rugby powerhouses.

The U19 side will complete RKS finals campaign when they take on Lelean Memorial School in another highly anticipated matchup.

For RKS, Saturday’s finals will not only be about chasing silverware but also showcasing the strength of its rugby development pathway, which continues to produce talented players across different age groups.

The school has built a proud rugby legacy over the years, and the opportunity to compete across multiple divisions provides another chance to reinforce that reputation.

Each match will present a different test, with traditional rivals QVS and Lelean standing in their way.

As the teams prepare for the final showdown at the HFC Bank Stadium, RKS will be hoping its depth across all grades can translate into a memorable finals performance and a successful day for the Tailevu-based school.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Chand’s comeback fuels World Championship
item.description: THE road to the 2026 WPA Heyball World Championship has been far from straightforward for Fiji’s top-ranked snooker player Nitesh Chand. After stepping away from the game for several years because of health issues, the 43-year-old South Seas Club, Lautoka player has battled his way back to the top.
item.content:encoded:
THE road to the 2026 WPA Heyball World Championship has been far from straightforward for Fiji’s top-ranked snooker player Nitesh Chand.

After stepping away from the game for several years because of health issues, the 43-year-old South Seas Club, Lautoka player has battled his way back to the top.

Now he is preparing to represent Fiji against the world’s best in Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia, at the Genting Highlands from 23rd till 26th of this month.

For Chand, the opportunity is the reward for more than two decades of dedication to a sport he has loved since first picking up a cue 26 years ago.

“I’ve practised so hard. I know I’ve practised so hard, and I have a good chance because I’ve been playing snooker for 26 years. This year I can see my form is coming back, so I must take advantage of this and produce good results,” Chand said

His return to elite competition carries even greater meaning after a difficult period that nearly ended his career.

“I had some health issues for two or three years and I left playing snooker,” he recalled.

“Then I made a strong comeback, winning a few tournaments and now I’m standing in the number one position in Fiji.”

Chand credits much of his resurgence to those who stood beside him during his toughest days, particularly his teammate Raymond Reddy.

“Raymond is the one who made me come back in this sport. Whatever I am today, it’s because of him.”

He also acknowledged his longtime coach, Suresh Chauhan, for shaping him into the player he is today, and also his sponsors Max Chandar and his doctor, Dr Neel for helping make his international campaign possible.

Despite entering a field stacked with professional players from around the world, Chand is embracing the challenge.

“There will be 64 men and 64 women competing. I know they are professionals while we are amateurs, but for sure I will give them a tough game.”

While his immediate focus is Malaysia, Chand is equally passionate about the future of snooker in Fiji.

Rejecting suggestions that snooker is a minor sport, he believes the game is growing rapidly, highlighted by local players now regularly producing century breaks.

He said international exposure would allow players to return home and share valuable knowledge with the next generation. South Seas Club is already planning coaching clinics for youngsters aged 14 and above, with the aim of nurturing future national representatives.

“Our plan is to develop the kids and let them play in Fiji before sending them overseas to compete and learn more.”

As Chand prepares to wear the Fiji colours once again, he hopes his journey from illness to the world stage will inspire others to chase their dreams, no matter the obstacles.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Nation unites | PM backs history-chasing Drua Women
item.description: PRIME Minister Sitiveni Rabuka has thrown his full support behind the McDonald’s Fijian Drua Women as they prepare for Saturday’s Super Rugby Women’s grand final, saying he will be at the ground to cheer on the side as it bids to create more history. Rabuka said he had closely followed the team’s campaign throughout the season and was confident the nation would rally behind the Drua.
item.content:encoded:
PRIME Minister Sitiveni Rabuka has thrown his full support behind the McDonald’s Fijian Drua Women as they prepare for Saturday’s Super Rugby Women’s grand final, saying he will be at the ground to cheer on the side as it bids to create more history.

Rabuka said he had closely followed the team’s campaign throughout the season and was confident the nation would rally behind the Drua.

“Well, I’ll be there to watch them. I’ve been watching most of their games,” he said.

“I wish them well, and we’re all behind them, as well as the national team, Flying Fijians wherever they’re playing.”

The Prime Minister’s endorsement comes as the Drua stand just 80 minutes away from adding another remarkable chapter to one of the greatest success stories in Super Rugby Women’s history.

The Fijian franchise took the competition by storm when it joined in 2022, winning the championship in its inaugural season before successfully defending the title in 2023 to become back-to-back champions in its first two years.

Those triumphs established the Drua as one of the competition’s powerhouse teams and showcased the quality of women’s rugby in Fiji.

The years that followed were more challenging as the Drua were unable to maintain the lofty standards they had set, missing out on another title while other teams closed the gap.

However, this season has seen a remarkable resurgence.

Victory on Saturday would see the McDonald’s Fijian Drua Women claim a third Super Rugby Women’s title.

With the backing of the Prime Minister and an expectant home crowd, the Drua will carry the hopes of a nation as they seek to reclaim the trophy.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: ‘A proud day for Fiji’
item.description: THE McDonald’s Fijian Drua Women head coach Mike Legge believes hosting the Super Rugby Women’s grand final in Fiji for the first time is a landmark achievement that extends far beyond his team. Speaking ahead of Saturday’s title decider against the NSW Waratahs, Legge described the occasion as a defining moment for women’s rugby in the country.
item.content:encoded:
THE McDonald’s Fijian Drua Women head coach Mike Legge believes hosting the Super Rugby Women’s grand final in Fiji for the first time is a landmark achievement that extends far beyond his team.

Speaking ahead of Saturday’s title decider against the NSW Waratahs, Legge described the occasion as a defining moment for women’s rugby in the country.

“It’s massive. Massive not just for the team, obviously massive for the country being a first time, but it’s just huge for women’s rugby in Fiji to be able to deliver something like this,” Legge said. “It’s a proud day for women’s sport in Fiji.”

The Drua earned hosting rights after defeating the Queensland Reds 31-5 in the home semi-final, setting up a third grand final meeting with the defending champions. Legge credited the players’ commitment throughout the season for putting the team in a position to create history.

“The girls have worked really hard. As I’ve said in the past, the girls work extremely hard. They deserve to be here in the final.”

He also highlighted the culture created within the squad as one of the biggest reasons behind their successful campaign. “I think one of the biggest keys to the success is just enjoying each other’s company, making sure the environment has been one of hard work but also a light and fun environment.

“I think with most teams, if the environment’s good, you’re going to get success.”

The Drua will now look to cap off their historic season by lifting the championship.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Flight club showcase
item.description: THE 2026 Flight Club Annual Showcase will provide a platform for Fiji’s young gymnasts to display their talents while raising funds for their upcoming international competition. Fiji Gymnastics Board director Maria Alvarado said the annual event is more than just an evening of performances, describing it as a celebration of the dedication, perseverance and achievements of Flight Club’s young athle
item.content:encoded:
THE 2026 Flight Club Annual Showcase will provide a platform for Fiji’s young gymnasts to display their talents while raising funds for their upcoming international competition.

Fiji Gymnastics Board director Maria Alvarado said the annual event is more than just an evening of performances, describing it as a celebration of the dedication, perseverance and achievements of Flight Club’s young athletes.

The showcase will be held on Saturday, August 1, at 5pm at the Palatium Kava Lounge, formerly the Globe Theatre, in Lautoka.

Alvarado said the event would serve as both a competition send-off and a fundraising initiative for the Flight Club Development Squad, which is preparing to compete at the National Clubs Carnival – Aerobic Gymnastics Competition on the Gold Coast in Australia.

She said the evening would allow family members, friends and the wider community to witness the progress of the gymnasts who have spent countless hours training and preparing for the prestigious competition.

“The showcase is a reflection of the hard work, discipline, teamwork and commitment shown by these young athletes as they prepare to proudly represent both Flight Club and Fiji on the international stage,” Alvarado said.

The programme will feature individual and multiple aerobic gymnastics routines from Levels 1 to 6, Youth Individual Male performances and AeroDance Division 1 routines.

Special guest performances will also be part of the event, with organisers keeping the identities of the performers under wraps until the night of the showcase.

Alvarado acknowledged the support of the club’s sponsors, particularly major sponsor Millbrook Hilljes Law Partners, whose contribution has significantly assisted the team’s preparations.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Legge calls for packed Park
item.description: THE McDonald’s Fijian Drua Women head coach Mike Legge has called on fans to turn Four R Stadium into a fortress when his side hosts the NSW Waratahs for the Saturday’s Super Rugby Women’s grand final this Saturday. The Drua enjoyed tremendous support during last weekend’s semi-final victory over the Queensland Reds, and Legge believes another capacity crowd can play a decisive role.
item.content:encoded:
THE McDonald’s Fijian Drua Women head coach Mike Legge has called on fans to turn Four R Stadium into a fortress when his side hosts the NSW Waratahs for the Saturday’s Super Rugby Women’s grand final this Saturday.

The Drua enjoyed tremendous support during last weekend’s semi-final victory over the Queensland Reds, and Legge believes another capacity crowd can play a decisive role.

“I think a packed-out Four R Stadium is going to make it really uncomfortable for the Waratahs, and that’s what we want,” Legge said.

Legge urged supporters to come out in force and continue lifting the players throughout the contest.

“We want our fans to come out in numbers, to come out in force and continue to lift and uplift the girls.”

He said crowd support often helped players respond during difficult periods of matches.

“The crowd play a massive part in actually bringing them back up to the level we need them.”

Despite the physical demands of a long season, Legge confirmed the Drua expected to field their strongest available squad.

“It’s the grand final, so I think both teams are managing bodies but we’re going to be full strength when we roll out on Saturday.”

Fans can purchase tickets online through the Drua’s ticketing platform, while tickets are also available at selected outlets across the country, including Prouds, Jack’s of Fiji and Shop N Save locations.

Ticket prices are from $5 for Grass Embankment, $10 for Grandstand and $15 for Platinum.

With history on the line, Legge hopes a sea of Drua supporters at the Four R Stadium that can help inspire the team to its first Super Rugby Women’s championship on home soil.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.
